_metadata.source: Hopkins Corridor Traffic and Placemaking Study — Workshop 2 Presentation
_metadata.description: Participant feedback from Workshop 2 small group discussions. Community members identified their top concerns by corridor segment. Values are approximate counts read from the stacked bar chart — not exact figures.
_metadata.status: APPROXIMATE — values estimated from chart visual, not from a data table. Use for illustrative purposes only. Label clearly as approximate in any UI.
_metadata.key_finding: Pedestrian Crossing Safety, Speeding, and Cyclist Safety were the three most commonly raised concerns — far outranking Parking Improvement, which ranked 9th of 16 issues. This reflects community priorities from the city's own public engagement process.
_metadata.caveat: Parking loss opposition grew significantly after Workshop 2 (March 2021) as the design proposals became more specific. This chart reflects early-process community priorities before parking removal was concretely proposed.

feedback_items.issue: Speeding Vehicles / Need for Traffic Calming
Perceived Usefulness of Traffic Calming Features

collision_data_workshop2.note: All-collisions figure — includes property damage only. Different from Workshop 4 injury/fatal subset.
collision_data_workshop2.comparison_note: Workshop 4 (March 2022) cited 18 injury/fatal collisions with 78% ped/cyclist involvement. Both figures are correct — Workshop 2 includes all collisions; Workshop 4 isolates the injury/fatal subset.

parking_retention_by_option.note: These are the parking retention ranges from the three design options presented at Workshop 2. The final approved design (May 2022) is a hybrid that achieved better parking retention on most segments.

mcgee_to_gilman.note: Most contested segment. Final approved design achieved better retention on McGee-Monterey block.

sutter_to_sonoma.note: Eastern residential segment — minimal parking impact under all options
